raw_case:
Delayed recovery of consciousness from anesthesia due to exacerbation of hydrocephalus caused by a ventriculoperitoneal shunt malfunction during general anesthesia in the prone position: a case report


Background

Dysfunction of ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunts can lead to decreased levels of consciousness. We report a case of delayed emergence from anesthesia due to the malfunction of a VP shunt during neurosurgery in the prone position.

Case presentation

A 75-year-old male with a history of VP shunt for a fourth ventricle obstruction underwent cerebral vascular anastomosis in the prone position. His preoperative level of consciousness was clear. The surgery under general anesthesia was completed without any particular issues. After discontinuation of anesthesia, the patient did not awaken for over an hour. Postoperative CT revealed exacerbated hydrocephalus, likely from VP shunt occlusion. After pumping the reservoir of the VP shunt, the patient regained consciousness. He was extubated and discharged from ICU on the second postoperative day with no neurological issues.

Conclusion

For surgical patients with a VP shunt, anesthesia management must consider the risk of shunt malfunction due to patient positioning.

Background

Hydrocephalus results from the distortion of normal cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) dynamics, leading to CSF accumulation and ventricular dilation [1]. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting, which reduces intracranial pressure by diverting CSF to the peritoneal cavity, is often used as the primary treatment for hydrocephalus [2]. VP shunts have been in use for many years, but despite significant advances in valve design and catheters, complications and failures occur relatively frequently [3]. VP shunts are prone to problems such as infection, migration, obstruction, and pseudocyst formation, leading to shunt failure and hydrocephalus [2–6].When performing surgery on a patient with a VP shunt, the location of the shunt and the flow of CSF should be considered. There have been reports on the perioperative management of patients with VP shunts undergoing surgery [7, 8]. Conversely, there are few reports on intraoperative shunt failure, hydrocephalus, or other complications in surgical patients with VP shunts.Here we report a case of shunt malfunction caused by prone positioning during neurosurgery, resulting in hydrocephalus and delayed awaking from anesthesia.

Case presentation

The patient is a 75-year-old man (161 cm, 63.5 kg) who visited a nearby clinic due to the onset of dizziness, gait disturbance, and cognitive impairment. Head CT and MRI revealed a tumor in the fourth ventricle and non-communicating hydrocephalus. A VP shunt with a reservoir and valve (proGAV™ shunt system, BBraun, Germany) was implanted to treat the hydrocephalus (Fig. 1), resulting in improvements in gait disturbance and cognitive function. Upon thorough examination, the patient was diagnosed with a thrombosed aneurysm of the right posterior inferior cerebellar artery. Aneurysm trapping and cerebral revascularization surgery were scheduled. The patient, who is on oral medication for diabetes, had no other cardiovascular complications.General anesthesia with total intravenous anesthesia was planned to monitor motor and somatosensory evoked potentials during surgery. Anesthesia induction was performed with propofol at an effect-site concentration of 3 µg/mL by target-controlled infusion, remifentanil at 0.2 µg/kg/min, fentanyl at 100 µg, and rocuronium at 40 mg. After tracheal intubation, the patient was placed in the prone position. Intraoperatively, TCI was used to adjust the propofol dosage and maintain a BIS value of 40–60. Respiratory and circulatory status remained stable during surgery. The operative time was 692 min, and the fluid balance was plus 2140 mL. After the surgery, spontaneous breathing resumed following the cessation of anesthetic agents, but the patient did not regain consciousness for over an hour. We did not detect any abnormal electroencephalogram findings on the BIS monitor. Blood gas analysis showed no significant abnormalities and body temperature was normal (37.0 °C). The possibility of a cerebrovascular event was considered. A head CT scan was performed with the patient remaining intubated. The CT scan revealed a worsening of hydrocephalus (Fig. 2a). It was hypothesized that the prone position had caused a VP shunt obstruction, exacerbating the hydrocephalus. Pumping the reservoir of the VP shunt resulted in the patient promptly opening his eyes and improving to a Glasgow Coma Scale of 9 (GCS, Eye, 4; Verbal, intubation; Motor, 4). The patient opened his eyes but was unable to follow commands and attempted to remove the tracheal tube himself. He was re-sedated with propofol and dexmedetomidine, with plans to awaken and extubate him after the hydrocephalus improved. The patient was admitted to the ICU, and the CSF outflow resistance on the VP shunt valve was changed from 14 to 10 cmH2O to increase CSF flow. The CT scan on the day after surgery showed insufficient improvement in hydrocephalus, so sedation was continued. During sedation management in the ICU, the hemodynamics were stable, and the respiratory condition was managed with pressure support ventilation (support pressure, 5 cmH2O; PEEP, 5 cmH2O; FiO2, 0.3), with a PaO2/FiO2 ratio of approximately 400 mmHg. The CT scan on the second postoperative day confirmed improvement in hydrocephalus (Fig. 2b), allowing us to stop sedation. After awakening, the patient could follow commands (GCS of 11, Eye,4; Verbal, intubation; Motor, 6), and no other neurological abnormalities were confirmed, so the patient was extubated and discharged from the ICU to the ward.

Discussion

We reported a case where prolonged prone positioning led to VP shunt obstruction, worsening hydrocephalus, and delayed emergence from anesthesia. There have been reports of increased intra-abdominal pressure during laparoscopic surgery causing VP shunt malfunction [9]. However, to our knowledge, no reports have identified intraoperative positioning as a factor in VP shunt obstruction.Several factors can induce delayed emergence from anesthesia [10, 11]. Residual effects of sedatives are unlikely to be the cause in this case because of intraoperative BIS values between 40 and 60, normal liver and renal function, and normal preoperative hypoalbuminemia or hypothermia. Blood gas data, glucose, and electrolytes were also within normal ranges. Based on differential diagnosis, we considered that a complication in the central nervous system might have occurred. Postoperative CT revealed hydrocephalus despite the presence of a VP shunt. To promote the flow of CSF, the unidirectional reservoir of the shunt was pumped. A few minutes after pumping the reservoir, the patient’s eyes opened. This suggests that the VP shunt might not have functioned intraoperatively, leading to hydrocephalus and delayed recovery of consciousness after the cessation of anesthetics.The detailed mechanisms of VP shunt occlusion remain unclear. The VP shunt may have kinked at the cervical level due to the forward flexion of the head during occipital surgery. Another possibility is that the prone position itself may have occluded the VP shunt tube. The VP shunt tube was placed subcutaneously in the anterior thoracic region (Fig. 1) and may have been compressed by the body. Other possibilities include increased intra-abdominal pressure due to the prone position, which may have obstructed the CSF flow.The preoperative CSF outflow resistance was set at 14 cmH2O. This value may have been appropriate because the patient’s preoperative level of consciousness was normal [12]. Therefore, it is unlikely that the CSF outflow resistance contributed to the delayed recovery of consciousness after anesthesia.While we had recognized that the patient had a VP shunt, the possibility of intraoperative obstruction or malfunction of the VP shunt due to the surgical position had not been considered. Previously, increased intra-abdominal pressure during laparoscopic surgery has been shown to induce malfunction of a VP shunt [9]. To avoid this complication, several reports have demonstrated the usefulness of transcranial Doppler for CSF flow monitoring [7, 8]. In the perioperative management of patients with VP shunts, it is important to consider the risk of shunt malfunction perioperatively, protect the shunt route, and monitor CSF flow with transcranial Doppler intraoperatively, depending on the situation.In conclusion, we reported a case of VP shunt malfunction and exacerbation of hydrocephalus during neurosurgery under prolonged prone positioning, which resulted in a delayed recovery of consciousness after anesthesia. It is important to manage anesthesia with the possibility of intraoperative shunt malfunctions in mind, depending on the position of the patient with a VP shunt implantation.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 75-year-old male, 161 cm, 63.5 kg.
- **Chief Complaint:** None stated (patient presented with dizziness, gait disturbance, and cognitive impairment prior to VP shunt placement, but no complaints immediately before surgery).
- **History of Present Illness:** Patient with a history of fourth ventricle obstruction and non-communicating hydrocephalus treated with a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt, leading to resolution of initial symptoms. Diagnosed with a thrombosed aneurysm of the right posterior inferior cerebellar artery, for which cerebral vascular anastomosis surgery was planned.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes mellitus (managed with oral medications).
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Preoperative level of consciousness was clear.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Preoperative imaging confirmed hydrocephalus treated by VP shunt. Intraoperative BIS monitoring showed adequate anesthesia depth (BIS 40–60). Postoperative head CT revealed worsened hydrocephalus.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration:** The delayed recovery of consciousness raised concerns for anesthetic-related causes, cerebrovascular events, or intracranial complications.
   - Residual effects of anesthetics ruled out due to appropriate BIS monitoring, normal liver and kidney function, and stable intraoperative and postoperative parameters.
   - No evidence of new neurological events (e.g., cerebrovascular accident) on imaging.
   - Postoperative CT confirmed exacerbated hydrocephalus, leading to suspicion of VP shunt malfunction.

2. **Evaluation of VP Shunt Malfunction:**
   - The prone surgical position likely caused obstruction of the VP shunt. Potential mechanisms included:
     - Kinking of the cervical shunt tubing due to forward neck flexion.
     - Compression of the thoracic portion of the shunt tubing by patient positioning.
     - Increased intra-abdominal pressure hindering CSF outflow.
   - Pumping the reservoir relieved the obstruction, and the patient's condition improved promptly, supporting VP shunt malfunction as the cause.

3. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - No metabolic disturbances (normal electrolyte levels, blood gases, and glucose).
   - No systemic complications identified (e.g., hypothermia or hypoalbuminemia).
   - Central nervous system imaging and response to pumping further ruled out alternative causes.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Delayed recovery of consciousness caused by exacerbation of hydrocephalus due to ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt obstruction during neurosurgery in the prone position.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Delayed recovery of consciousness due to hydrocephalus from VP shunt malfunction.

checked_rare_disease: Hydrocephalus with stenosis of the aqueduct of Sylvius

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by going through the case step by step. The patient is a 75-year-old male with a history of hydrocephalus treated with a VP shunt. He had a thrombosed aneurysm in the right PICA and was scheduled for cerebral vascular anastomosis. After the surgery, the CT showed worsened hydrocephalus. 

First, I need to consider why the hydrocephalus got worse postoperatively. The VP shunt was previously effective, so maybe there's a problem with the shunt now. Shunt malfunction is common—could be a blockage, disconnection, or infection. But since the surgery was for the aneurysm, maybe the procedure itself caused some complications.

Wait, the aneurysm was in the posterior inferior cerebellar artery. Surgical manipulation in that area might lead to swelling or bleeding, which could obstruct CSF pathways again. The fourth ventricle was previously obstructed, so any new obstruction could re-trigger hydrocephalus. Alternatively, maybe the shunt isn't working anymore. But the shunt was placed earlier and resolved symptoms, so maybe the recent surgery caused an issue with the shunt's function.

Another angle: the patient had a vascular anastomosis. Could there be a hemorrhage or edema post-surgery contributing to increased intracranial pressure and hydrocephalus? The CT showed worsened hydrocephalus, so imaging would show enlarged ventricles. But why? If the shunt was working, the ventricles should be smaller. So either the shunt failed, or there's another reason for CSF accumulation.

Also, the patient has diabetes, which could affect healing or infection risk. But there's no mention of infection signs here. So more likely a mechanical issue with the shunt. Maybe during the aneurysm surgery, the shunt was accidentally kinked or displaced? Or perhaps the surgery caused some scarring or obstruction in the CSF pathways again.

Wait, the original hydrocephalus was due to fourth ventricle obstruction (non-communicating). If the VP shunt was working, then the recent surgery might have caused a new obstruction. Alternatively, the thrombosed aneurysm might have led to a cerebellar infarct or edema, compressing the fourth ventricle. But the aneurysm was thrombosed, so maybe it was a chronic issue. However, surgical intervention in that area could have caused acute changes.

Another possibility is normal pressure hydrocephalus, but the patient had a non-communicating type before. The VP shunt was placed for that. Post-op CT showing worse hydrocephalus suggests that the shunt is not functioning properly. So the most likely diagnosis is VP shunt malfunction leading to recurrent hydrocephalus. But why did it malfunction? Maybe due to the recent surgery—positioning, mechanical disruption, or intraoperative factors. Alternatively, the shunt could have failed independently, but the timing post-surgery suggests a connection.

Alternatively, the vascular anastomosis might have altered blood flow, but I'm not sure how that would directly cause hydrocephalus. The primary issue here is the worsened ventricles on CT after surgery. So the conclusion would be that the VP shunt is not functioning, causing recurrent hydrocephalus. Therefore, the final diagnosis is VP shunt malfunction causing worsened hydrocephalus postoperatively.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient had a VP shunt placed for non-communicating hydrocephalus due to fourth ventricle obstruction, which initially resolved symptoms. Postoperative head CT after cerebral vascular anastomosis surgery revealed worsened hydrocephalus, indicating recurrence of CSF accumulation despite prior shunt placement.

<step 2> Shunt malfunction is a common complication of VP shunts, often due to obstruction, disconnection, or infection. The temporal association with the recent vascular surgery raises suspicion for mechanical disruption (e.g., intraoperative shunt displacement or kinking) or obstruction secondary to surgical manipulation near the posterior fossa.

<step 3> Alternative causes include new obstruction of CSF pathways (e.g., due to postoperative edema, hemorrhage, or scarring from the aneurysm surgery) or impaired CSF absorption. However, the shunt’s prior efficacy and sudden postoperative worsening strongly suggest shunt failure as the primary issue.

<step 4> The absence of immediate infection signs (e.g., fever, leukocytosis) and the lack of reported intraoperative complications (e.g., hemorrhage) further support mechanical shunt dysfunction as the most likely etiology for recurrent hydrocephalus.

### Answer:
VP shunt malfunction causing recurrent hydrocephalus.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient had a VP shunt placed for non-communicating hydrocephalus due to fourth ventricle obstruction, which initially resolved symptoms. Postoperative head CT after cerebral vascular anastomosis surgery revealed worsened hydrocephalus, indicating recurrence of CSF accumulation despite prior shunt placement.

Shunt malfunction is a common complication of VP shunts, often due to obstruction, disconnection, or infection. The temporal association with the recent vascular surgery raises suspicion for mechanical disruption (e.g., intraoperative shunt displacement or kinking) or obstruction secondary to surgical manipulation near the posterior fossa.

Alternative causes include new obstruction of CSF pathways (e.g., due to postoperative edema, hemorrhage, or scarring from the aneurysm surgery) or impaired CSF absorption. However, the shunt’s prior efficacy and sudden postoperative worsening strongly suggest shunt failure as the primary issue.

The absence of immediate infection signs (e.g., fever, leukocytosis) and the lack of reported intraoperative complications (e.g., hemorrhage) further support mechanical shunt dysfunction as the most likely etiology for recurrent hydrocephalus.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial concerns were raised about anesthetic-related causes, cerebrovascular events, or intracranial complications due to delayed recovery of consciousness.
Residual effects of anesthetics were ruled out due to appropriate BIS monitoring, normal liver and kidney function, and stable intraoperative and postoperative parameters.
No evidence of new neurological events was found on imaging.
Postoperative CT confirmed exacerbated hydrocephalus, suggesting VP shunt malfunction.

The prone surgical position likely caused obstruction of the VP shunt, potentially through kinking of the cervical shunt tubing, compression of the thoracic portion of the shunt tubing, or increased intra-abdominal pressure hindering CSF outflow.
Pumping the reservoir relieved the obstruction, and the patient's condition improved promptly, confirming VP shunt malfunction as the cause.
No metabolic disturbances were identified (normal electrolyte levels, blood gases, and glucose).
No systemic complications were found (e.g., hypothermia or hypoalbuminemia).
Central nervous system imaging and response to pumping further ruled out alternative causes.
Final diagnosis: Delayed recovery of consciousness caused by exacerbation of hydrocephalus due to ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt obstruction during neurosurgery in the prone position.
